name: Aitvaras
description: Serpent with grass snake head, able to change forms and fly. Strong animal that wanders woodlands. Can protect families and bring prosperity.
appearance: Serpent with grass snake head, can change forms. Can grow to large size when protective.
behavior: Can be convinced to protect families and bring prosperity. Connected to thunder god Perkūnas. Brought good luck before Christianization.
cultural_significance: Lithuanian dragon tradition. Associated with prosperity, thunder, meteorites. Important in Lithuanian folklore.
summary: Serpent with grass snake head, able to change forms and fly
fun_fact: In some traditions, Aitvaras can be convinced to protect families and bring prosperity.
seo_description: Serpent with grass snake head, able to change forms and fly. Strong animal that wanders woodlands. Can protect families and bring prosperity.
source: Lithuania mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Baubas
description: Shadowy presence in dark corners, under beds. Pulls hair, presses on chest. Used to discipline children. Domestic fear figure.
appearance: Shadowy presence. Dark, threatening.
behavior: Pulls hair, presses on chest. Disciplines children. Domestic fear.
cultural_significance: Lithuanian household demon. Child discipline figure.
summary: Shadowy presence in dark corners, under beds
fun_fact: In some traditions, Baubas is said to pull hair, presses on chest.
seo_description: Shadowy presence in dark corners, under beds. Pulls hair, presses on chest. Used to discipline children. Domestic fear figure.
source: Lithuania mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Dievas
description: Supreme sky god in Lithuanian mythology, creator of the universe.
appearance: Radiant sky figure
behavior: Creator deity, rules the cosmos.
cultural_significance: Supreme Baltic deity
summary: Supreme sky god in Lithuanian mythology, creator of the universe
fun_fact: In some traditions, Dievas is said to creator deity, rules the cosmos.
seo_description: Supreme sky god in Lithuanian mythology, creator of the universe.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Garcis (or Gartis)
description: Latvian giant, similar to Lithuanian 'Gigantis'. Very strong but not very smart. Created hills and valleys by accident.
appearance: Huge humanoid, long beard, wears simple animal skins. Very strong-looking but gentle expression.
behavior: Works on large earthworks accidentally created features. Can be friendly but clumsy. Often outwitted by humans.
cultural_significance: Baltic giant folklore. Explains geological features. Similar to European giants.
summary: Latvian giant, similar to Lithuanian 'Gigantis'
fun_fact: In some traditions, Garcis (or Gartis) is said to work on large earthworks accidentally created features.
seo_description: Latvian giant, similar to Lithuanian 'Gigantis'. Very strong but not very smart. Created hills and valleys by accident.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Giltinė
description: Death goddess with a long venomous tongue in Lithuanian folklore.
appearance: Hooded figure with tongue
behavior: Collects souls of the dead.
cultural_significance: Lithuanian death deity
summary: Death goddess with a long venomous tongue in Lithuanian folklore
fun_fact: In some traditions, Giltinė is said to collect souls of the dead.
seo_description: Death goddess with a long venomous tongue in Lithuanian folklore.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Kratt
description: Creature formed from hay or old household implements by a master who gave their soul to the Devil. Does everything commanded, used for stealing.
appearance: Formed from hay or old implements, takes shapes as commanded. Can appear human-like or as various objects.
behavior: Does everything commanded, used for stealing and bringing goods. Can fly. If given impossible job, becomes enraged and flies as bolide.
cultural_significance: Estonian magical tradition. Related to Germanic Kobold. Featured in Estonian literature and film "November."
summary: Creature formed from hay or old household implements by a master who gave their soul to the Devil
fun_fact: In some traditions, Kratt is said to does everything commanded, used for stealing and bringing goods.
seo_description: Creature formed from hay or old household implements by a master who gave their soul to the Devil. Does everything commanded, used for stealing.
source: Estonia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Kräkl (or Kratt)
description: Artificial creature created by a sorcerer from hay, wood, or household objects. Must be given a mouthful of its creator's blood to come alive. Does chores but can turn against maker if neglected.
appearance: roughly humanoid shape made from hay, straw, wood, rags. No face, just a bundle with arms and legs.
behavior: Works tirelessly but cannot speak. Must be kept busy or it causes chaos. Can be destroyed by burning or if it learns its creator's secret name.
cultural_significance: Folkloric version of a golem. Explains mysterious farm work and warnings about dark magic.
summary: Artificial creature created by a sorcerer from hay, wood, or household objects
fun_fact: In some traditions, Kräkl (or Kratt) is said to work tirelessly but cannot speak.
seo_description: Artificial creature created by a sorcerer from hay, wood, or household objects. Must be given a mouthful of its creator's blood to come alive. Does ch
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Kulmking
description: Chilling spirit of unholy dead, arising from souls of wicked individuals. Deeply tied to forests and bogs. Shapeshifts, punishes those who disrespect nature.
appearance: Chilling, shapeshifting. Spirit of wicked dead.
behavior: Punishes those who disrespect nature. Shapeshifts. Spirit of unholy dead.
cultural_significance: Estonian forest spirit. Represents moral nature enforcement.
summary: Chilling spirit of unholy dead, arising from souls of wicked individuals
fun_fact: In some traditions, Kulmking is said to punishes those who disrespect nature.
seo_description: Chilling spirit of unholy dead, arising from souls of wicked individuals. Deeply tied to forests and bogs. Shapeshifts, punishes those who disrespect
source: Estonia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Lauma (Latvian)
description: Female nature spirit and goddess of fate, childbirth, and meadows in Latvian mythology.
appearance: Beautiful woman with horse features, or old crone with healing powers.
behavior: Helps with childbirth, protects children, aids farmers. Can curse if disrespected.
cultural_significance: Important Baltic goddess of fertility and destiny.
summary: Female nature spirit and goddess of fate, childbirth, and meadows in Latvian mythology
fun_fact: In some traditions, Lauma (Latvian) is said to help with childbirth, protects children, aids farmers.
seo_description: Female nature spirit and goddess of fate, childbirth, and meadows in Latvian mythology.
source: Latvia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Lauma (Latvian)
description: Female nature spirit and goddess of fate, childbirth, and meadows in Latvian mythology.
appearance: Beautiful woman with horse features, or old crone with healing powers.
behavior: Helps with childbirth, protects children, aids farmers. Can curse if disrespected.
cultural_significance: Important Baltic goddess of fertility and destiny.
summary: Female nature spirit and goddess of fate, childbirth, and meadows in Latvian mythology
fun_fact: In some traditions, Lauma (Latvian) is said to help with childbirth, protects children, aids farmers.
seo_description: Female nature spirit and goddess of fate, childbirth, and meadows in Latvian mythology.
source: Lithuania mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Laumas (plural: Laumės)
description: Similar to Lithuanian laumė - female forest and water spirits. Can help or hinder humans depending on how they're treated.
appearance: Beautiful women, sometimes with bird feet or other animal features. Wearing white or green.
behavior: Guardians of nature. Help farmers, protect wildlife. Can be tricksters. Demands respect.
cultural_significance: Baltic nature spirits. Syncretic with Christian saints in some areas.
summary: Similar to Lithuanian laumė - female forest and water spirits
fun_fact: In some traditions, Laumas (plural: Laumės) is said to guardian of nature.
seo_description: Similar to Lithuanian laumė - female forest and water spirits. Can help or hinder humans depending on how they're treated.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Laumė
description: Female nature spirit or fairy in Lithuanian folklore. Can be beautiful and helpful, or old and dangerous. Protector of animals and nature.
appearance: Beautiful woman with long blonde hair, wearing white or green. Can also appear as old hag to scare people.
behavior: Helpful to kind people, punish the rude. Often appear as beautiful young women with long hair. Can be mischievous.
cultural_significance: Lithuanian nature spirits. Female figures similar to Slavic 'rusalka' but less deadly.
summary: Female nature spirit or fairy in Lithuanian folklore
fun_fact: In some traditions, Laumė is said to helpful to kind people, punish the rude.
seo_description: Female nature spirit or fairy in Lithuanian folklore. Can be beautiful and helpful, or old and dangerous. Protector of animals and nature.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Libahunt
description: Estonian werewolf from souls of immoral individuals. Takes wolf form through curse, learned magic, or ritual devices. Active near thresholds and forest edges.
appearance: Wolf form. Werewolf transformation.
behavior: Takes wolf form through various means. Active near property lines and forest edges.
cultural_significance: Estonian werewolf tradition. Represents moral transgression.
summary: Estonian werewolf from souls of immoral individuals
fun_fact: In some traditions, Libahunt is said to takes wolf form through various means.
seo_description: Estonian werewolf from souls of immoral individuals. Takes wolf form through curse, learned magic, or ritual devices. Active near thresholds and fores
source: Estonia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Metsavana
description: The 'Old Man of the Forest' in Estonian mythology, also called metsataat or metsaisa. Each forest has its own Metsavana who rules over all its creatures and decides the hunters' harvest. He can speak with birds and animals and governs the forest's bounty. Associated with the Finno-Ugric forest spirit tradition connecting to Slavic Leshy and Komi Vörsa.
appearance: Tall elderly man of large build, eyes full of life's wisdom. Beard overgrown with white moss. Clothes of spruce bark resembling bark itself, birch hat and boots. Very active and youthful in spirit despite age.
behavior: Rules the forest, decides whether hunters find berries and game. Can mislead those who disrespect nature or guide respectful travelers. Protects wild animals—bears, wolves, snakes, foxes. Very agile despite elderly appearance. Plays tricks on the lost.
cultural_significance: Central Estonian nature deity representing the forest's living essence. Shows animist connection to land in Finno-Ugric tradition. Part of complex spirit hierarchy including Metsaema (forest mother) and metsahaldjas (forest fairy). Used in metsiku tegemine festivals.
summary: The 'Old Man of the Forest' in Estonian mythology, also called metsataat or metsaisa
fun_fact: In some traditions, Metsavana is said to rules the forest, decides whether hunters find berries and game.
seo_description: The 'Old Man of the Forest' in Estonian mythology, also called metsataat or metsaisa. Each forest has its own Metsavana who rules over all its creatur
source: Estonia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Näkk
description: Estonian variant of Nordic nixie, a shapeshifting water spirit with subtypes. Lures victims with enchanting violin music and appears as a horse, treasure, or handsome man to drown the unwary.
appearance: Handsome young man playing violin, or beautiful horse with water weeds in mane.
behavior: Lures people with violin music. Shapeshifts into a horse, treasure, or seductive human. Drags victims into water.
habitat: Lakes, rivers, bogs, coastal waters in Estonia
cultural_significance: Widespread water spirit reflecting Estonia’s thousands of lakes and strong water-related folklore tradition.
summary: Estonian variant of Nordic nixie, a shapeshifting water spirit with subtypes
fun_fact: In some traditions, Näkk is said to lures people with violin music.
seo_description: Estonian variant of Nordic nixie, a shapeshifting water spirit with subtypes. Lures victims with enchanting violin music and appears as a horse, treas
source: Estonia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Perkūnas
description: Thunder god in Lithuanian mythology, comparable to Thor and Zeus.
appearance: Powerful thunder figure
behavior: Rules thunder and lightning.
cultural_significance: Second most important Baltic deity
summary: Thunder god in Lithuanian mythology, comparable to Thor and Zeus
fun_fact: In some traditions, Perkūnas is said to rules thunder and lightning.
seo_description: Thunder god in Lithuanian mythology, comparable to Thor and Zeus.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Põrgupõrgu (Hell)
description: Estonian hell/demon realm. Creatures from there can enter our world. Often associated with dragons and evil spirits.
appearance: Demonic - red skin, horns, tail, glowing eyes. Traditional demon appearance.
behavior: Demonic beings that tempt or harm humans. Can possess people, cause disasters.
cultural_significance: Christian influence on Estonian paganism. Represents evil and temptation.
summary: Estonian hell/demon realm
fun_fact: In some traditions, Põrgupõrgu (Hell) is said to demonic beings that tempt or harm humans.
seo_description: Estonian hell/demon realm. Creatures from there can enter our world. Often associated with dragons and evil spirits.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Pūķis
description: Mischievous household spirit in Latvian folklore.
appearance: Small imp-like figure
behavior: Steals from neighbors, brings wealth to master.
cultural_significance: Part of Latvian folklore
summary: Mischievous household spirit in Latvian folklore
fun_fact: In some traditions, Pūķis is said to steal from neighbors, brings wealth to master.
seo_description: Mischievous household spirit in Latvian folklore.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Ragana
description: Witch goddess of prophecy in Lithuanian mythology.
appearance: Witch woman with moon
behavior: Prophecy, magical powers.
cultural_significance: Important pagan deity
summary: Witch goddess of prophecy in Lithuanian mythology
fun_fact: In some traditions, Ragana is said to prophecy, magical powers.
seo_description: Witch goddess of prophecy in Lithuanian mythology.
source: Baltic mythology, collected folk songs (dainas)

name: Sumpurnis
description: Permanent predator wrapped in dense, shaggy fur - bear-like humanoid with man's torso and dog's head. Insatiable hunger drives attacks on humans.
appearance: Bear-like humanoid with man's torso and dog's head. Dense shaggy fur, powerful build.
behavior: Attacks humans - tears flesh, devours, drinks blood. Can be created by wizards binding a belt. Extraordinarily strong.
cultural_significance: Latvian equivalent to werewolf tradition. Represents dangerous predators and wizard transformations.
summary: Permanent predator wrapped in dense, shaggy fur - bear-like humanoid with man's torso and dog's head
fun_fact: In some traditions, Sumpurnis is said to attack humans - tears flesh, devours, drinks blood.
seo_description: Permanent predator wrapped in dense, shaggy fur - bear-like humanoid with man's torso and dog's head. Insatiable hunger drives attacks on humans.
source: Latvia mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections
